text: Today we are going to present a fluorescence-guided total robotic parietal peritonectomy, cytotoxic surgery and HIPEC for an advanced disease. Peritoneal surface malignancy is a disseminated disease which is present with multiple nodules all over the peritoneal cavity above the shoulder loss. The commonest cause of peritoneal surface malignancy other than primary is ovarian colorectal with carcinomatosis peritonei which warrants advanced disease. Cytorectomy surgery as shown here present with multiple nodules all over the peritoneum cavity which needs ten to twelve hours of surgery to do this complex. A forty-five-year-old premenopausal lady with stage IIIc advanced ovarian CA with peritoneal carcinomatosis society's momentum presented to us. Biopsy showed high-grade carcinoma. Tumor board advised neoadjuvant chemotherapy. Cytorectomy surgery peritonectomy and then HIPEC. As shown in this post-NACCT, CA-one-twenty-five normalized with a good response.

The surgical team applies the ERAS protocol, including pre-carb loading, before proceeding with multi-quadrant surgery involving the entire abdomen. The lower abdomen is docked for pelvic peritonectomy, total parietal peritonectomy, and pouch otoglossectomy, followed by upper abdomen procedures. Cautery settings are adjusted, and five hours prior, IV ICG is administered, which remains in tumor tissue while washing off from scar tissue. Peritonoscopy is performed, and PCI indexing with ICG highlights extensive disease in the diaphragm peritoneum, Glisson's capsule, omentum, parietal peritoneum, and pouch otoglass, with a PCI index of fifteen.

The surgeon begins by using a scissor with monopolar cautery to carefully dissect the lateral peritoneal valve, ensuring the rectus sheath and posterior transverse fascia are not injured. The fat is kept down, and the inferior epigastric vessels are preserved. The parietal peritoneum is removed like a parachute on the left side, exposing the psoas and genitofemoral nerve. Retroperitoneal structures are visible, and ICG-guided excision is used to remove nodes, with APC laser applied for hemostasis. Large nodes from the Rasper-Lyons trial are excised. Sheets of peritoneum are removed, connected with the lateral line of Toldt. The left ovarian pedicle is addressed, and the ureter is identified using ICG Firefly with a robotic vessel sealer. The ovarian pedicle is taken high in the Okabayashi area, preserving nerves. The surgeon addresses the pouch of Douglas, where deposits are present, and connects the right lateral pelvic peritoneum to the peritonectomy, guarding the ureter and retroperitoneal vessels. The appendix and ileocecal area peritoneum are also excised.

The surgeon preserves the hypogastric nerve to maintain sexual and bladder function. The pouch of Douglas is cleared of disease through Aldrich's procedure, separating it from the rectal nodule. The bare fibers of the rectum are visible. Cannulas are exchanged for instruments, and a hot shear is used for peritonectomy. The psoas muscle vessels and genitofemoral nerve are exposed, and the pelvic bladder peritoneum is removed.

The surgeon performs an appendicectomy with nodules using a SureForm stapler to achieve optimal CC-0 cytoreduction. Specimens are bagged. The procedure transitions to Aldrich's procedure, where the bladder is distended with a Foley catheter for easier bladder peritonectomy, connecting to the vasocovaginal septum. Care is taken to preserve and clear Okabayashi's, Yukuba's, and Latzow's triangles. Sharp scissors are used to remove the entire mesorectum and the pouch of Douglas peritoneum with disease. The uterine arteries are clamped and cut at the internal iliac origin, and ICG-guided lymph node clearance is performed on the right side.

The surgeon develops two spaces, paravesical and pararectal, between the operator hypogastric and external iliac, and ureter and internal iliac. Using a 30-degree inverting camera, the urachus, umbilicus, anterior peritoneal cavity, and bladder peritoneum are visualized. The bladder stripping is performed, separating the entire peritoneal sheath from the bladder, which is clamped and distended. The pelvis is exposed as a parachute sheet, with the bladder and rectal fibers appearing bare. An early colpotomy is performed at three and nine o'clock, followed by extra-peritoneal handling of uterine vessels using a vessel sealer. The nerves and spaces of Lezo and Ocobio are addressed, completing Aldrich's procedure with a parachute-like pouch otoglossectomy.

The surgeon passes an endobag through the vagina to contain the specimen. Docking is performed in the upper abdomen, and a special instrument called the pedjet is used to suspend the stomach. A total supra colic omentectomy and omental barsectomy are performed, starting from the foramen of Winslow and extending to the lesser omentum, to remove diseased tissue while safeguarding the stomach and the arch under the pancreatic capsule. The right subdiaphragmatic area and peritoneum are completely stripped, preserving the muscle as heart shears touch it, causing the muscle to retract. The bare area of the coronary ligament is addressed.

The surgeon clips the lymphatic duct cisterna and retrieves specimens from the retroperitoneal para-aortic nodes near the renal veins on the vena cava and aorta using hot shears. Hypertonic saline irrigation is applied to the port site subcutaneously, and the closed technique of HIPEC is performed using a dedicated machine.
